title: SEGMENT 5 — The pre-IND meeting — Type B, Type C, and the "are we on the right path" check

lines.text: SEGMENT 5 — The pre-IND meeting — Type B, Type C, and the "are we on the right path" check
So you have done your preclinical work.
You have a vector.
You have biodistribution and tox data.
You have, in your head and in a thousand-page binder, a plan for how you would dose the first human.

Before you actually file the IND, you have a choice: you can file cold, dropping a giant binder on the agency and hoping they don't put you on clinical hold; or you can request a meeting and find out, in advance, what the agency thinks of your plan.
Almost every serious sponsor takes the meeting.
Filing cold is a way to get put on clinical hold for thirty days while the agency works through concerns that a conversation could have resolved in an hour.
These pre-IND conversations have a structured format.
FDA divides sponsor meetings into a small number of types, named with letters.
The two types you care about for the pre-IND phase are Type B and Type C.
A Type B meeting is what you request when you want to discuss specific, well-defined milestones in the development of your drug.
The pre-IND meeting itself is a Type B meeting.
So is an end-of-Phase-2 meeting, where you discuss whether your Phase 2 data is sufficient to design the Phase 3 trial.
So is a pre-BLA meeting, where you discuss the structure of your BLA submission.
Type B meetings have a regulatory expectation of a sixty-day turnaround from request to meeting, plus written responses from the agency.
They are the workhorse format for big formal program-level discussions.
A Type C meeting is broader — used for any other discussion about a drug under development that doesn't fit the Type B categories.
Sponsors use Type C meetings for things like discussing a novel manufacturing change, or a new biomarker, or a specific scientific question that doesn't tie to a particular milestone.
Type C meetings have a longer expected turnaround, seventy-five days, but they are flexible enough to accommodate almost any question.
The pre-IND meeting itself — almost always a Type B — is, I think, the single most important conversation in the early life of a gene therapy program.
You go to the agency with a briefing document that summarizes your preclinical data, your proposed clinical protocol, your manufacturing plan, and the specific questions you want them to address.
The questions are usually some version of: is our preclinical package adequate?
Is our proposed starting dose reasonable?
Is our trial design acceptable?
Are there additional studies you would want to see before we file?
The agency, on its side, has reviewed your briefing document in advance.
The meeting itself is short — typically an hour — and is structured around your specific questions.
The agency gives you their preliminary answers, often hedged with caveats, and then sends you written responses formalizing what was said.
Those written responses become the regulatory record of the meeting.
You can refer back to them when you file the IND.
If you did what the agency told you to do at the pre-IND meeting, and the IND reviewer disagrees with the pre-IND advice, you have a basis to push back.
Let me give you the analogy.
The pre-IND meeting is the "are we on the right path" check.
Imagine you are about to embark on a long hike through unfamiliar terrain.
You have a map.
You have a route planned.
Before you set off, you stop at the ranger station and you ask the ranger: does this look like a reasonable route?
Are there any hazards along this trail I should know about?
Is my equipment adequate for the conditions?
The ranger has seen many hikers attempt many routes.
They know which trails have washed out, which rivers are running high, which bears are active this time of year.
Their advice is not binding — they cannot stop you from hiking the route you planned — but if you ignore it, you do so at your own risk.
And if you take their advice and something still goes wrong, you have at least the cover of having checked in advance.
The pre-IND meeting is exactly that, in regulatory form.
The agency is the ranger.
They have seen hundreds of programs come through.
They know what kinds of preclinical packages tend to clear and what kinds tend to get held up.
They know which trial designs have caused problems in past gene therapy trials.

Their advice is not a guarantee — they reserve the right to change their mind once they see the full IND — but it is enormously valuable, and the sponsors that engage with it tend to have smoother regulatory paths than the sponsors that try to navigate alone.
One last note.

The pre-IND meeting is also, increasingly for gene therapy, the venue where you discuss whether your program might qualify for one of the accelerated pathways — Fast Track, Breakthrough, RMAT — that we will talk about in Segment 7.
The agency can give you a preliminary view of whether your program looks like a fit for one of those designations.
The designations themselves are formal regulatory grants that come later, but the conversation about fit often starts at the pre-IND meeting.
Many gene therapy sponsors leave their pre-IND meeting with a clear sense of which accelerated pathway they should aim for, and they design the rest of their program around qualifying for it.
